name: Jollof Rice
description: Nigeria's most celebrated dish, smoky, richly spiced tomato rice cooked to perfection in a single pot. A staple at every party and gathering.
ingredients: Salt and seasoning cubes to taste
instructions: Blend tomatoes, red bell peppers, scotch bonnet and half the onion into a smooth paste.
Heat oil in a wide pot over medium-high heat. Fry the blended pepper mix for 15–20 minutes, stirring regularly, until the tomato loses its raw smell and the oil floats to the top.
Add tomato paste and fry for another 5 minutes.
Pour in chicken stock and season with curry powder, thyme, bay leaves, salt, and seasoning cubes.
Wash the rice thoroughly and add to the pot. Stir to coat every grain in the sauce.
Add just enough water so the liquid sits 2cm above the rice level.
Cover with foil, then place the lid on top. Cook on low heat for 30–35 minutes without lifting the lid.
After 30 minutes, open and check, the water should be absorbed and the bottom slightly smoky. Stir gently from the bottom and serve.

name: Egusi Soup
description: A rich, deeply flavoured soup made from ground melon seeds, leafy greens, and assorted meats. Best enjoyed with pounded yam or fufu.
instructions: Boil the assorted meat and stockfish with one onion, seasoning cubes, and salt until tender. Set aside, reserving the stock.
Heat palm oil in a pot over medium heat. Add the remaining chopped onion and fry for 2 minutes.
Mix ground egusi with a little water to form a thick paste. Add to the hot oil and fry, stirring, for 10 minutes until the egusi clumps form golden-brown nuggets.
Add the blended pepper and crayfish. Stir and cook for 5 minutes.
Pour in the meat stock (about 2 cups). Add the cooked meats and stockfish.
Cover and simmer for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add the washed, chopped spinach or bitter leaf. Cook for a final 5 minutes.
Adjust seasoning and serve with pounded yam, fufu, or eba.

name: Pounded Yam
description: The king of Nigerian swallows, smooth, stretchy pounded yam made from boiled white yam, eaten with virtually any Nigerian soup.
instructions: Peel the yam, cut into chunks, and rinse under cold water.
Place in a pot, cover with water, and boil until very soft (about 25–30 minutes). A fork should slide through with no resistance.
Drain the water completely. Transfer the hot yam to a mortar.
Pound firmly with a pestle, turning and folding the yam between strokes. Add a splash of water if it feels too stiff.
Continue pounding for 10–15 minutes until the yam is completely smooth with no lumps, and has a stretchy, dough-like consistency.
Alternatively, use a food processor or stand mixer with a dough hook for 8–10 minutes on medium speed.
Mould into balls and serve immediately with any Nigerian soup.

name: Moi Moi
description: A soft, savoury steamed bean pudding made from blended black-eyed peas and spices. Incredibly versatile, serve as a side, a snack, or a full meal.
instructions: Soak the beans in water for 30 minutes. Rub vigorously between your palms to remove the outer skins. Rinse until all skins are removed.
Blend the peeled beans with the peppers, onion, and a little water until completely smooth, smoother than you think is necessary.
Pour into a bowl. Add oil, seasoning cubes, and salt. Mix thoroughly.
The batter should be pourable but thick, like pancake batter. Adjust water accordingly.
Prepare your moulds, you can use small tins, foil cups, or uma leaves.
Pour batter into each mould, filling two-thirds. If using eggs or fish, add a piece to each mould.
Place in a pot with 3cm of boiling water at the bottom. Steam on medium heat for 45–50 minutes, adding water as needed.
Test with a skewer, it should come out clean. Leave to cool slightly before unmoulding.

name: Suya
description: Smoky, spiced skewered beef coated in groundnut powder and grilled over open flame. Nigeria's most beloved street food, sold across every city after dark.
ingredients: 800g beef sirloin or rump, thinly sliced
4 tablespoons groundnut (peanut) powder, toasted and finely ground
instructions: Mix all dry spices and seasoning cube together in a bowl to make the suya spice (yaji). This is the heart of suya.
Thread the thin slices of beef onto metal or soaked wooden skewers.
Press the suya spice all over both sides of the meat, coating generously.
Leave to marinate for at least 30 minutes (or overnight in the fridge for best results).
Grill over hot charcoal or on a very hot grill pan, brushing with oil, for about 4–5 minutes per side.
The meat should have a slightly charred exterior while remaining juicy inside.
Serve hot with thinly sliced raw onions, tomatoes, and extra yaji powder on the side.
Wrap in newspaper or foil in true street-food style.

name: Akara
description: Crispy on the outside, soft and fluffy inside, these deep-fried black-eyed pea fritters are a beloved Nigerian breakfast, especially with ogi (pap) or bread.
instructions: Soak beans for 20 minutes, then rub off all the outer skins. Rinse thoroughly.
Blend the peeled beans with the peppers and onion using very little water. The batter should be thick, not watery.
Season with salt and crumbled seasoning cube. Whisk the batter vigorously for 5 minutes to incorporate air, this makes them fluffy.
Heat enough oil for deep frying in a pot to 175°C (medium-high).
Drop spoonfuls of batter carefully into the hot oil. Do not overcrowd.
Fry for 3–4 minutes, turning once, until golden brown all over.
Drain on paper towels and serve immediately with pap (ogi), custard, or freshly baked bread.

name: Puff Puff
description: Soft, pillowy, slightly sweet deep-fried dough balls that are impossible to stop eating. Found at every Nigerian party, road-side, and school gate.
instructions: Combine flour, yeast, sugar, salt, and nutmeg in a bowl.
Add warm water gradually and mix until you have a smooth, slightly sticky batter. It should be thicker than pancake batter.
Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and leave in a warm place for 45 minutes to 1 hour until the batter doubles in size and is bubbly.
Heat oil in a deep pot to 175°C (medium heat). The oil should be deep enough to submerge the puff puff.
Using wet fingers or a spoon, scoop small balls of batter and drop gently into the oil.
Fry for 4–5 minutes, turning halfway, until deep golden brown all over.
Drain on paper towels. Serve warm as-is or dusted with powdered sugar.

name: Amala
description: Smooth, dark, slightly chewy swallow made from dried yam flour. The Yoruba staple, always paired with ewedu and gbegiri for the ultimate combination.
instructions: Bring 3 cups of water to a rolling boil in a pot.
Reduce heat to medium-low. Gradually sprinkle in the yam flour while stirring continuously with a wooden spoon.
Stir vigorously in circular motions, breaking any lumps immediately. The amala should start pulling away from the sides.
Add small amounts of hot water to adjust consistency, it should be smooth, slightly firm, and stretchy.
Cover and leave on very low heat for 5 minutes, stirring once midway.
Mould into smooth balls with a wet wooden spoon and serve immediately with ewedu, gbegiri, or any Nigerian soup.

name: Ewedu Soup
description: A silky, gently mucilaginous jute leaf soup that's light, fast to cook, and full of minerals. Always served alongside gbegiri and amala in the famous 'abula' combination.
instructions: Pick the ewedu leaves off the stems and wash thoroughly.
Place in a small pot with 1 cup of water and the potash (if using). Boil for 5–7 minutes until completely softened.
Use a broom whisk (ijabe) or an immersion blender to break down the leaves, you want a slightly slimy, stringy texture, not fully smooth.
Add ground crayfish, seasoning cube, and salt.
Simmer for 3 more minutes, stirring.
Serve immediately alongside gbegiri stew and amala, or with any preferred swallow.

name: Gbegiri
description: A smooth, earthy bean soup made from skinned brown beans and palm oil. Essential to the classic Yoruba 'abula' trio with amala and ewedu.
instructions: Soak the beans for 30 minutes, then rub off the skins. Rinse well.
Boil the beans with onion until very soft and almost mushy, about 30 minutes.
Blend or mash the beans with their cooking water until completely smooth.
Heat palm oil in a pot, add blended peppers and crayfish. Fry for 5 minutes.
Pour in the blended bean mixture. Add seasoning cubes and salt.
Stir well and simmer on low heat for 10 minutes until thick and velvety.
Serve warm alongside ewedu and amala.

name: Oha Soup
description: A prized Igbo soup made with tender oha leaves and cocoyam paste as a thickener. Rich with assorted meats and crayfish, it's a taste of Igbo heritage.
instructions: Cook the assorted meat and stockfish with one seasoning cube until tender. Reserve the stock.
Boil the cocoyams until soft, peel, and pound or blend into a smooth paste.
Heat palm oil in a pot, add the blended peppers and fry for 5 minutes.
Add the meat stock, meats, and stockfish. Add crayfish, ogiri, and remaining seasoning cube.
Drop the cocoyam paste in small lumps into the soup. Stir gently and let it dissolve to thicken the soup.
Simmer for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add the hand-shredded oha leaves (do not cut, tearing preserves flavour). Stir and cook for 3–5 minutes.
Adjust seasoning and serve with pounded yam or fufu.

name: Nsala Soup
description: A clean, pale, intensely flavoured Igbo soup made without palm oil. Catfish and uziza leaves give it a distinctive aroma. Light on colour, heavy on taste.
instructions: Boil and pound or blend the cocoyam until smooth. Set aside.
In a pot, bring water to boil. Add the catfish, crayfish, whole peppers, and seasoning cubes.
Cook for 15 minutes until the fish is nearly done.
Add the cocoyam paste in small balls. Stir and simmer for 10 minutes until the soup thickens and turns a light cream colour.
Add the uziza and utazi leaves. Stir gently.
Simmer for 3–5 minutes. Adjust salt.
Serve with pounded yam, this soup is especially perfect with it.

name: Nkwobi
description: A rich, spiced Igbo delicacy of cow foot in a palm kernel sauce with fermented oil bean seeds. Often eaten as a nighttime treat with palm wine or beer.
instructions: Ensure the cow foot is cooked until completely tender. Drain and set aside.
Mix palm kernel oil with a small amount of potash solution and stir vigorously until it turns yellow and creamy.
Add ground crayfish, ehuru, seasoning cube, and blended scotch bonnet. Mix thoroughly.
Add the cow foot pieces and ugba to the sauce. Mix well to coat everything.
Taste and adjust seasoning.
Garnish with shredded utazi leaves.
Serve warm or at room temperature in a clay pot if available.

name: Abacha
description: A vibrant Igbo 'salad' made from dried cassava strips soaked and dressed in palm oil sauce with ugba, garden eggs, and spices. Served cold, refreshing and satisfying.
instructions: Soak the abacha in cold water for 10–15 minutes until softened. Drain and set aside.
Mix the palm oil with the potash solution in a bowl, stir until it turns yellow and creamy. This is the dressing.
Add ground crayfish and salt to the dressing. Mix well.
Add the drained abacha to the dressing and toss to coat.
Add the ugba, sliced peppers, onion rings, and garden egg slices.
Toss everything together gently. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Garnish with shredded utazi leaves.
Serve at room temperature or slightly chilled.

name: Tuwo Shinkafa
description: A soft, smooth, mould-able swallow from Northern Nigeria made from over-cooked sticky rice. Mild in flavour and perfect with any soup.
instructions: Rinse the rice and place in a pot with the water. Cook uncovered on medium-high heat.
Stir regularly as the rice cooks and begins to absorb water.
Once the water is nearly absorbed, lower the heat and continue stirring vigorously.
Mash and pound the rice continuously with the back of a heavy spoon until it becomes completely smooth, sticky, and cohesive.
The tuwo should pull cleanly from the pot sides. Add a little water if needed to adjust.
Shape into smooth balls while still hot. Serve immediately with miyan kuka, egusi, or any thick soup.

name: Miyan Kuka
description: A dark, earthy, distinctly flavoured Hausa soup made from powdered baobab leaves and dry fish. Served with tuwo shinkafa or tuwo masara.
instructions: Cook the meat until tender with one onion and a seasoning cube. Reserve the stock.
Heat groundnut oil in a pot. Fry the remaining onion and blended peppers for 5 minutes.
Add the meat stock and cooked meat. Bring to a boil.
Stir in the kuka powder gradually, whisking to avoid lumps.
Add dry fish, crayfish, dawadawa, and remaining seasoning cube.
Simmer on low heat for 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally. The soup will thicken and turn dark.
Adjust seasoning. Serve with tuwo shinkafa.

name: Kilishi
description: Nigeria's answer to beef jerky, thinly pounded, spiced, and sun-dried meat coated in a paste of groundnut, spices, and pepper. A northern delicacy sold in sheets.
instructions: Slice the beef very thinly against the grain, about 3mm thick.
Pound the slices flat with a meat mallet to about 5mm even thickness.
Mix groundnut paste with all spices, crushed seasoning cubes, and salt to form a thick marinade.
Coat the flattened beef liberally on both sides with the marinade.
Lay out on wire racks and sun-dry for 2–3 days in strong sun, turning once daily. Alternatively, oven-dry at 70°C for 4–5 hours.
Grill briefly over hot charcoal just before serving to crisp and char the edges.
Store in an airtight container. Keeps for weeks.

name: Masa
description: Soft, spongy rice pancakes with crispy edges cooked in a special pan with round moulds. A beloved Hausa breakfast eaten with suya, miyan taushe, or honey.
instructions: Blend the soaked raw rice with the cooked rice and a little water until completely smooth.
Add sugar, yeast, and salt. Mix well.
Leave the batter in a warm place for 1–2 hours to ferment and rise.
Heat a masa pan (or mini muffin/aebleskiver pan) and brush each mould with oil.
Pour batter to three-quarters full in each mould.
Cover and cook on medium-low heat for 5–7 minutes until the tops are set and the bottoms are golden.
Serve warm with miyan taushe, suya, or a drizzle of honey.

name: Ofada Rice and Ayamase
description: Earthy, aromatic local Ofada rice served with the notorious Ayamase, a smoky, spicy green pepper stew made with bleached palm oil and assorted offal.
ingredients: 4 tablespoons bleached palm oil (fried to clear)
instructions: Cook the ofada rice until done, it will stay slightly chewy and earthy. Set aside.
Blend all peppers coarsely, you want some texture remaining.
Heat the bleached palm oil in a pot until it smokes lightly and clears (this is important for the Ayamase flavour).
Add the blended peppers carefully and fry on high heat, stirring constantly for 15–20 minutes.
Add the cooked assorted meats, crayfish, iru, and seasoning cubes.
Reduce heat and simmer for 20 minutes until the oil floats to the top and the stew is thick.
Serve the ofada rice wrapped in banana leaves with the Ayamase stew poured generously on top.

name: Efo Riro
description: A vibrant, lightly spiced Yoruba vegetable soup built around stir-fried shoko or spinach leaves. Rich with assorted meat and prawn, it is one of the healthiest Nigerian soups.
ingredients: 3 red bell peppers and 3 scotch bonnet (blended)
instructions: Cook assorted beef until tender. Set aside.
Heat palm oil in a wide pot. Fry blended peppers with onion and iru for 10 minutes.
Add crayfish, seasoning cubes, and salt.
Add the cooked beef, smoked fish, and prawns. Stir and simmer 5 minutes.
Add the shredded vegetables. Stir-fry briskly for 5 minutes, do not overcook. The leaves should stay bright green.
Adjust seasoning. Serve with eba, semolina, or pounded yam.

name: Ofe Akwu
description: A richly fragrant palm nut soup from the Niger Delta, thick, orange-red, and perfumed with banga spices. Best served with starch or pounded yam.
ingredients: 2kg fresh palm nuts (or 1 tin palm cream)
instructions: If using fresh palm nuts: boil for 30 minutes, pound to extract the cream, sieve out fibres and seeds.
Pour the palm cream into a pot. Add a cup of water and bring to boil.
Add the cooked meats, dried fish, blended peppers, and crayfish.
Add the banga spice mix and onion.
Season with seasoning cubes and salt.
Simmer uncovered for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the soup thickens and oil separates on top.
Add shredded scent leaves in the last 5 minutes.
Serve with pounded yam or starch.

name: Eba
description: Nigeria's most affordable and accessible swallow, made from granulated cassava (garri) mixed with hot water. Quick, filling, and pairs with any soup.
instructions: Bring water to a rolling boil in a pot.
Gradually pour the garri into the boiling water while stirring constantly with a strong wooden spoon.
Keep stirring and turning, the eba should gather into a stiff, smooth ball.
Cover for 2 minutes. Then stir again, adding a splash of boiling water if too stiff.
The perfect eba is smooth, firm, and slightly stretchy — not grainy.
Mould into neat balls and serve immediately with any soup.

name: Pepper Soup
description: A fiery, aromatic broth made with pepper soup spice blend and your choice of fish, goat meat, or offal. Believed to cure colds, hangovers, and clear sinuses.
ingredients: 1kg catfish or goat meat (cut into pieces)
3 tablespoons pepper soup spice mix (uziza seeds, ehuru, uda, utazi)
Uziza leaves or scent leaves for garnish
instructions: Clean and season the fish or meat with onion, seasoning cube, and salt.
Place in a pot with enough water to cover. Bring to boil.
Add the pepper soup spice mix, whole scotch bonnet peppers, and crayfish.
Cook on medium heat for 25–30 minutes, fish cooks faster than meat.
Taste and adjust with salt and more pepper soup spice.
The broth should be clear but deeply flavoured and very aromatic.
Garnish with fresh uziza or scent leaves.
Serve as a starter or with agidi (cornstarch pudding).

name: Boli and Groundnut
description: Charred, smoky roasted plantain served with raw or roasted groundnuts. The ultimate Nigerian street snack — smoky, sweet, salty, and completely addictive.
instructions: Keep the plantains in their skins. Grill directly over hot charcoal or a gas flame, turning every 5 minutes.
Cook for 15–20 minutes until the skin is completely blackened and the inside is soft.
While the plantain grills, toast the groundnuts in a dry pan, stirring constantly, until golden and fragrant.
Season groundnuts lightly with salt if desired.
Peel the plantain (or serve unpeeled for the authentic roadside experience).
Serve together — alternate bites of sweet smoky plantain with crunchy salty groundnut.

name: Fried Plantain
description: Caramelised, sweet, golden-fried ripe plantain slices. A Nigerian staple served at breakfast, beside rice, beans, or just on its own as a snack.
ingredients: 2 very ripe plantains (black-spotted for best sweetness)
instructions: Peel the plantains and cut diagonally into thick slices (about 1.5cm).
Heat oil in a pan to medium — enough to come halfway up the slices.
Fry in batches for 2–3 minutes per side until deep golden-brown and caramelised.
Do not rush, medium heat ensures they cook through without burning.
Drain on paper towels. Sprinkle with a tiny pinch of salt if desired.
Serve immediately alongside rice and stew, beans, or eggs.

name: Okra Soup
description: A thick, silky okra soup with a satisfying 'draw' texture. Loaded with seafood and meat, it is best eaten with eba or pounded yam.
instructions: Cook the beef until tender. Add the seafood in the last 5 minutes. Set aside.
Heat palm oil in a pot. Fry blended peppers and onion for 5 minutes.
Add the meat stock, crayfish, ogiri, and seasoning cubes. Bring to simmer.
Add the cooked meats and seafood.
Add the finely sliced okra all at once. Stir quickly, do not cook too long.
Simmer for 5–7 minutes. The okra will release its characteristic mucilage, thickening the soup.
Adjust seasoning. Serve immediately with eba or pounded yam.

name: Nigerian Beans and Plantain
description: Soft, mashed honey beans served with a deeply spiced palm oil sauce (Ewa Agoyin), paired with golden fried plantain. A complete, affordable, and incredibly satisfying meal.
ingredients: 2 cups honey beans (oloyin) or black-eyed peas
instructions: Cook beans with a pinch of salt until very soft and beginning to break down. Mash roughly.
For the Agoyin sauce: heat palm oil until it smokes and clears. Allow to cool slightly.
Add the ground dry peppers, onion, and iru to the cooled oil. The mixture will sizzle intensely.
Fry on medium heat for 20 minutes, stirring until the sauce becomes dark and fragrant.
Add crayfish and seasoning. Pour the sauce over the mashed beans.
Fry the plantain slices until golden and caramelised.
Serve beans topped with the Agoyin sauce alongside the fried plantain.

name: Yam and Egg Sauce
description: Fluffy boiled yam served alongside a quick, spiced egg sauce with tomatoes, peppers, and onions. The most reliable Nigerian breakfast, quick, filling, and comforting.
instructions: Peel and cut the yam into thick slices or wedges. Boil in salted water for 20–25 minutes until fork-tender. Drain.
While yam cooks, heat oil in a frying pan over medium heat.
Fry onion until translucent, then add diced tomatoes and peppers.
Cook the tomato base for 8 minutes until softened and the raw smell is gone.
Season with the seasoning cube and salt.
Beat the eggs and pour over the tomato mixture. Scramble gently, stirring, until just set but still moist.
Serve immediately alongside the boiled yam.

name: Ogi
description: Smooth, creamy fermented corn porridge, Nigeria's most comforting breakfast. Served warm with milk and sugar, or alongside akara and moi moi.
ingredients: 1 cup corn starch (ogi/akamu, wet or dried)
instructions: If using wet ogi: dilute with cold water to a smooth, lump-free consistency.
Bring 2 cups of water to boil in a pot.
Mix the remaining cold water with the ogi paste until completely smooth.
Pour the cold mixture slowly into the boiling water while stirring constantly and quickly.
Stir vigorously for 3–5 minutes as the ogi cooks and thickens.
The consistency should be silky and pourable, not too thick, not too watery.
Sweeten with sugar, pour a swirl of evaporated milk on top.
Serve hot alongside akara, moi moi, or on its own.

name: Miyan Taushe
description: A vibrant, mildly spiced Hausa pumpkin soup thickened with groundnut paste. Served with tuwo shinkafa and loved across Northern Nigeria.
instructions: Cook meat with onion and seasoning until tender. Reserve the stock.
In a pot, heat groundnut oil and fry blended tomatoes and peppers for 8 minutes.
Add the pumpkin pieces and the meat stock. Cook for 10 minutes.
Stir in the groundnut paste dissolved in a little water.
Add crayfish, cooked meat, and remaining seasoning cube.
Simmer for 15 minutes until the pumpkin is soft and the soup is thick.
Add the green leaves and cook for 3 minutes.
Serve with tuwo shinkafa or masa.

name: Nigerian Jollof Spaghetti
description: Spaghetti cooked jollof-style in a rich, spiced tomato base. A Nigerian-Italian fusion that needs no introduction, loved by kids and adults alike.
instructions: Par-boil the spaghetti for 5 minutes, drain, and set aside.
Heat oil in a wide pot. Fry onion then add blended tomatoes.
Add tomato paste, curry, thyme, seasoning cubes, and salt. Fry for 10 minutes.
Add the cooked chicken or sliced sausages. Stir.
Pour in enough water for the pasta to cook, about 2 cups.
Add the par-boiled spaghetti. Stir gently to coat.
Cover and cook on low heat for 15–20 minutes until the pasta absorbs the sauce and is fully cooked.
Serve with fried plantain or coleslaw.

name: Ofe Onugbu
description: A bold, slightly bitter Igbo soup made with washed bitter leaves and thickened with cocoyam. Deep, earthy, and best eaten with pounded yam.
instructions: Cook assorted meat and stockfish until tender. Reserve stock.
Boil cocoyam until soft, peel, and pound smooth for thickening.
Heat palm oil in a pot. Add blended peppers and fry for 5 minutes.
Add meat stock, meats, crayfish, ogiri, and seasoning cubes.
Add cocoyam lumps. Stir and simmer for 15 minutes.
Add the washed bitter leaf. Cook for 10–15 minutes.
Adjust seasoning. Serve with pounded yam or fufu.

name: Chin Chin
description: Addictively crunchy, lightly sweetened fried dough strips or balls. A mainstay at Nigerian parties, packed in bags at roadside stands, and given to children as treats.
instructions: Rub butter into the flour until it resembles fine breadcrumbs.
Add sugar, nutmeg, baking powder, and salt. Mix.
Add beaten eggs and milk. Mix into a stiff dough, do not overwork.
On a floured surface, roll dough thin (about 3mm). Cut into strips or small shapes.
Heat oil to medium (170°C). Fry in batches until golden and crisp, about 5–7 minutes.
Drain on paper towels. They will continue to harden as they cool.
Store in an airtight tin once completely cooled.

name: Tomato Stew
description: The cornerstone of Nigerian cooking, a deep, rich tomato and pepper stew that pairs with rice, yam, beans, bread, or any swallow. Every Nigerian household has a pot of this.
instructions: Season the chicken with onion, seasoning cube, curry, thyme, and salt. Cook until done.
Blend tomatoes, bell peppers, scotch bonnet, and half the onion.
Heat oil in a pot until hot. Add the remaining diced onion and fry briefly.
Pour in the blended tomato mixture. Fry on medium-high heat for 25 minutes, stirring regularly, until thick and the oil rises to the top.
Add tomato paste and fry for 5 more minutes.
Add the cooked chicken and the stock. Season with bay leaves and adjust salt.
Simmer for 15 minutes. Serve over rice, with yam, or with bread.

name: Groundnut Soup
description: A rich, nutty soup made from roasted groundnuts and spiced with tomatoes and peppers. Creamy, filling, and packed with protein, perfect with rice or any swallow.
instructions: Cook meat until tender. Reserve stock.
Blend tomatoes, peppers, and onion.
In a pot, heat a little oil and fry the blended tomato base for 8 minutes.
Add meat stock and the cooked meat.
Dilute the groundnut paste with water to a smooth consistency and stir into the pot.
Add crayfish and seasoning cubes. Stir well.
Simmer on medium-low heat for 20 minutes, stirring often to prevent sticking.
Adjust thickness with water. Serve with rice or swallow.

name: Nigerian Fried Rice
description: Nigerian fried rice is nothing like Chinese fried rice. It's richly spiced, colourful with vegetables, and cooked in seasoned chicken liver stock, a party showstopper.
ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (carrots, green beans, sweet corn, green peas)
instructions: Cook chicken liver in seasoned water (curry, thyme, seasoning) for 15 minutes. Reserve the stock. Dice the liver.
In the liver stock, parboil the rice for 10 minutes. The stock should just cover the rice. Drain.
Heat oil in a wide pot or wok on high heat. Add onion and fry briefly.
Add the parboiled rice. Stir-fry briskly for 5 minutes.
Add the diced vegetables and liver. Stir fry another 5 minutes.
Add soy sauce, curry powder, and seasoning. Keep stir-frying until the rice is dry and every grain is coated.
Add scrambled egg pieces if using. Serve with coleslaw and chicken.

name: Banga Rice
description: Rice cooked directly in palm nut cream with banga spices, a one-pot dish from the Niger Delta that is deeply fragrant and unforgettably rich.
ingredients: 1 tin palm cream (or 1.5kg fresh palm nuts, cooked and extracted)
instructions: Pour palm cream into a pot. Add 2 cups water and bring to boil.
Add cooked assorted meat, crayfish, banga spice, and seasoning.
Add the washed parboiled rice. Stir to combine.
The liquid should be about 3cm above the rice level. Add more water if needed.
Cover and cook on medium-low heat for 25–30 minutes until the rice is done and fragrant.
Add shredded scent leaves in the final 3 minutes.
Stir gently and serve.

name: Vegetable Soup
description: The most celebrated soup from Cross River, a lavish, lush combination of ugu (fluted pumpkin) and waterleaves with assorted seafood and meat, swimming in palm oil.
instructions: Cook all meats and stockfish until tender. Reserve the stock.
Blanch the water leaves briefly, drain well, and squeeze out excess water.
Heat palm oil in a wide pot. Add iru and blended peppers. Fry for 5 minutes.
Add the meat stock, meats, dried fish, periwinkle, and crayfish.
Season and simmer for 10 minutes.
Add the squeezed water leaves. Stir and cook for 5 minutes.
Add the ugu leaves. Stir and cook for final 3–5 minutes. Do not overcook.
Serve with pounded yam, eba, or fufu.

name: Ogbono Soup
description: A thick, hearty, 'drawing' soup made from ground ogbono (wild mango) seeds. One of Nigeria's most popular soups, comforting, filling, and pairs with any swallow.
instructions: Cook meat and dried fish until tender. Reserve stock.
Dissolve ground ogbono in the warm palm oil in a pot, stirring over medium heat for 3 minutes.
Add the meat stock gradually, stirring continuously to prevent lumps.
Add meats, crayfish, blended peppers, iru, and seasoning. Stir.
Simmer for 20 minutes, the ogbono will draw and thicken the soup beautifully.
Add the shredded leaves. Cook for 5 minutes.
Serve with eba, pounded yam, or fufu.

name: Afang Soup
description: A premium Efik soup featuring the distinctively bitter afang (okazi) leaves paired with water leaves, assorted seafood, and generous palm oil.
instructions: Cook all meats, stockfish, and dried fish until tender. Reserve stock.
Blend the scotch bonnet peppers.
Heat palm oil in a pot, add iru and blended peppers. Fry 5 minutes.
Add stock, meats, periwinkle, crayfish, and seasoning. Simmer 10 minutes.
Squeeze excess water from water leaves and add to pot. Cook 5 minutes.
Add the finely sliced afang leaves. Stir and cook 5 minutes only, do not overcook.
Adjust seasoning. The soup should be quite thick.
Serve with pounded yam, eba, or fufu.

name: Baked Chicken
description: Whole chicken pieces marinated in Nigerian spices then baked to crispy golden perfection. A party staple that rivals any fried chicken.
instructions: Score the chicken pieces deeply with a knife to allow marinade to penetrate.
Mix all spices, seasoning, grated onion, and oil into a paste.
Rub the marinade all over the chicken pieces, working into the scored cuts.
Marinate for at least 2 hours, or overnight for best flavour.
Preheat oven to 200°C.
Place chicken on a wire rack over a baking tray. Bake for 45–50 minutes.
Turn once halfway and baste with any drippings.
The chicken is done when the juices run clear and the skin is deeply golden.
Serve with jollof rice or fried rice.

name: Nigerian Coleslaw
description: The creamy, bright-yellow Nigerian party coleslaw, a must-have alongside fried rice and chicken. Tangy, sweet, and unmistakably Nigerian.
instructions: Shred the cabbage as finely as possible. Place in a colander, sprinkle with salt, and leave for 10 minutes. Squeeze out excess water.
Combine the cabbage, grated carrot, sweetcorn, and onion in a large bowl.
In a separate bowl, mix mayonnaise, salad cream, and sugar.
Pour the dressing over the vegetables and toss thoroughly.
Taste and adjust seasoning.
Chill for at least 30 minutes before serving for best flavour.
Serve cold alongside fried rice and chicken.

name: Coconut Rice
description: Fragrant, mildly sweet rice cooked in coconut milk with spices. A simple, beautiful dish that elevates everyday rice into something special.
instructions: Wash the rice until the water runs clear.
In a pot, fry the onion and garlic in a little oil for 2 minutes.
Pour in the coconut milk and water. Add curry powder, seasoning, and salt.
Add the washed rice and stir.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to the lowest setting.
Cover tightly and cook for 20–25 minutes without lifting the lid.
Check, the liquid should be absorbed. Fluff gently and serve.

name: Bread and Ewa Agoyin
description: Soft Agege bread dipped into thick, peppery Ewa Agoyin bean sauce, the ultimate Lagos street breakfast. Cheap, filling, and deeply satisfying.
ingredients: 1 loaf Agege bread (or soft white bread)
1 serving Ewa Agoyin sauce (see Beans and Plantain recipe)
instructions: Prepare the Ewa Agoyin sauce (mashed honey beans + dark fried palm oil sauce).
Cut the Agege bread into thick slices or tear into chunks.
Serve the mashed beans in a bowl, topped generously with the hot Agoyin sauce.
Dip, scoop, and eat the bread with the beans, messily and joyfully.

name: Nigerian Salad
description: Nigeria's famous layered party salad, boiled potatoes, eggs, carrots, and green vegetables bound in mayonnaise and decorated with precision. Every party must have it.
ingredients: 1 tin baked beans or green peas
instructions: Boil diced potatoes and carrots separately until just cooked. Cool completely.
Mix potatoes, carrots, sweetcorn, and beans together. Season lightly.
Fold in mayonnaise until everything is coated.
Lay lettuce leaves in a wide serving bowl.
Mound the potato mixture on top.
Decorate with sliced boiled eggs, cucumber, and tomato arranged artfully.
Chill for 1 hour before serving.

name: Hausa Koko
description: A warming, spiced millet porridge from Northern Nigeria with ginger, cloves, and pepper, full of flavour and energy for the morning. Served with kose (bean fritters).
instructions: Blend millet flour with ginger, cloves, and black pepper with a little water to a smooth slurry.
Bring 3 cups of water to boil in a pot.
Slowly pour the millet mixture into the boiling water while stirring briskly.
Continue stirring to prevent lumps. Reduce heat to low.
Cook for 10–15 minutes, stirring regularly, until the porridge is smooth and fragrant.
Sweeten with sugar. Add evaporated milk if desired.
Serve hot with masa or kose.

name: Noodle Soup
description: The unofficial meal of every Nigerian student and busy person, instant noodles elevated with eggs, vegetables, and the signature seasoning sachets.
instructions: Boil water in a small pot. Add the noodles and cook for 2 minutes.
Drain most of the water, leaving just a little in the pot.
Add the seasoning sachets. Stir to coat the noodles.
Push noodles to the side, add oil, and scramble the eggs in the same pot.
Mix eggs through the noodles.
Top with sliced peppers and spring onions.
Serve immediately.

name: Isi Ewu
description: A highly prized Igbo delicacy, goat head cooked in a spiced palm kernel sauce with ehuru and utazi. A dish of celebration, respect, and communal gathering.
ingredients: 1 goat head (cleaned and cut into pieces)
instructions: Clean the goat head thoroughly. Cook in seasoned water for 60–90 minutes until the meat is completely tender.
Mix palm kernel oil with potash solution until it turns yellow and creamy.
Add crayfish, ehuru, and blended peppers to the sauce. Mix well.
Add the cooked goat head pieces to the sauce. Toss to coat thoroughly.
Place in a pot and warm through on low heat for 15 minutes.
Garnish with utazi leaves.
Serve in a clay pot as tradition demands.

name: Nigerian Oatmeal
description: Quick-cook oats spiced the Nigerian way, with a little nutmeg, served with milk and sugar. Simple, nutritious, and on the table in 10 minutes.
instructions: Bring water (or milk) to boil in a small pot.
Add oats, nutmeg, and salt. Stir.
Cook on medium heat for 3–5 minutes, stirring frequently, until creamy.
Add sugar and stir.
Serve hot with a generous pour of evaporated milk on top.
Top with sliced banana or groundnuts if desired.

name: Plantain Porridge
description: A hearty, comforting pottage made from unripe plantain, palm oil, and fish. One-pot, affordable, and deeply satisfying.
instructions: Peel plantains and cut into rounds or chunks.
Place in a pot. Add enough water to just cover the plantain.
Add palm oil, blended peppers, onion, crayfish, smoked fish, and seasoning.
Cook on medium heat for 20 minutes until the plantain is soft.
Mash some pieces with the back of a spoon to thicken the sauce.
Add shredded scent leaves. Cook for 3 more minutes.
Adjust seasoning and serve.

name: Yam Porridge
description: Tender yam chunks in a rich palm oil sauce with peppers and fish. A wonderfully comforting, filling one-pot meal that is quick to make.
instructions: Place the yam cubes in a pot. Add water to cover.
Add palm oil, blended peppers, onion, crayfish, smoked fish, and seasoning.
Cook on medium heat for 25–30 minutes until the yam is completely soft.
Use a spoon to mash some of the yam into the sauce to thicken it.
Add shredded scent leaves and cook for 3 more minutes.
Adjust seasoning. Serve hot.

name: Okpa
description: A dense, savoury steamed pudding from Enugu made from bambara nut flour, firm, filling, and eaten as a morning snack or light meal.
ingredients: Banana leaves or foil for wrapping
instructions: Mix okpa flour with warm water to a smooth, pourable batter.
Add palm oil, pepper, seasoning, and salt. Mix well.
The batter should be pourable, like thin pancake batter.
Pour into banana leaf cups or foil cups, filling two-thirds.
Steam in a covered pot with simmering water for 45–50 minutes.
It is done when firm and a skewer comes out clean.
Serve warm as a snack or with pap.

name: Ofe Owerri
description: A rich, special-occasion Igbo soup from Owerri, cocoyam-thickened and loaded with a wide variety of meats, fish, and greens.
ingredients: Uziza leaves and oha leaves mixed
instructions: Cook all meats and fish. Reserve stock.
Heat palm oil. Add blended peppers and ogiri. Fry 5 minutes.
Add stock and all cooked meats.
Add crayfish and seasoning. Simmer 10 minutes.
Drop pounded cocoyam in small balls. Stir to dissolve and thicken.
Simmer 15 minutes.
Add the mixed leaves. Cook 5 minutes.
Serve with pounded yam.

name: Nigerian Scrambled Eggs
description: Nigerian-style scrambled eggs with tomatoes, peppers, and onions. Ready in 10 minutes and served with bread or boiled yam.
instructions: Heat oil in a pan. Fry onion until translucent.
Add diced tomatoes and scotch bonnet. Cook for 5 minutes.
Beat eggs with a pinch of salt and pour into the pan.
Stir gently until just set but still moist.
Serve with bread or boiled yam.

name: Dambu Nama
description: Finely shredded, fried, spiced beef, a Hausa delicacy with a texture similar to meat floss. Served as a snack or used to garnish tuwo and soups.
instructions: Boil the beef until very tender with onion, seasoning, and salt.
Drain. Allow to cool completely.
Shred the beef very finely using two forks or your fingers.
Season shredded beef with ginger, garlic, and pepper. Toss well.
Fry in batches in hot oil, stirring constantly, until golden and dry.
Drain on paper towels. The dambu will crisp up as it cools.
Store in an airtight container.

name: Fried Yam
description: Golden, slightly crispy deep-fried yam, one of the most popular Nigerian snacks and street foods. Eaten plain or dipped in pepper sauce.
instructions: Peel yam and cut into thick sticks or rounds.
Parboil the yam in salted water for 5 minutes. Drain and dry completely.
Heat oil in a deep pot to 175°C.
Fry yam in batches for 5–7 minutes until golden and crisp on the outside.
Drain on paper towels.
Season with salt. Serve with Nigerian pepper sauce or ketchup.

name: Tuwo Masara
description: A firm, smooth swallow made from finely ground maize flour. The northern Nigerian staple that costs almost nothing and fills you completely.
instructions: Bring 3 cups of water to boil.
Mix the maize flour with 1 cup of cold water to a smooth paste.
Pour the paste slowly into boiling water, stirring continuously.
Stir vigorously, breaking all lumps, on medium heat for 10 minutes.
Cover and cook on low for 10 more minutes, stirring every 2 minutes.
The tuwo should be very firm and smooth. Add a little water if needed.
Serve with miyan kuka, miyan taushe, or any Hausa soup.

name: Zobo Drink
description: A vibrant, tart, refreshing hibiscus drink spiced with ginger, cloves, and orange peel. Nigeria's most beloved homemade drink, served at parties and sold across the country.
instructions: Rinse the zobo leaves thoroughly.
Boil water in a large pot. Add zobo leaves, ginger, cloves, cinnamon, and fruit peel.
Boil for 20 minutes. The liquid will turn deep burgundy-red.
Remove from heat. Allow to cool for 30 minutes.
Strain through a fine sieve, pressing to extract all liquid.
Add pineapple juice if using. Sweeten with sugar to taste.
Chill completely before serving over ice.

name: Kunun Gyada
description: A creamy, mild peanut milk drink from Northern Nigeria, nutritious, filling, and served cold as a breakfast drink or mid-day refreshment.
instructions: Blend roasted groundnuts with 2 cups of water until completely smooth.
Strain through a fine cloth or sieve, squeezing to extract all the milk.
Mix the strained milk with the remaining water.
Add sugar, salt, and vanilla. Mix well.
Chill thoroughly before serving.
Serve cold over ice.

name: Nigerian Pepper Steak
description: Tender beef strips stir-fried in a bold Nigerian pepper sauce, quick to make and full of flavour. Serve with rice, yam, or as a party small chop.
instructions: Season beef with seasoning cube, garlic powder, and salt. Marinate for 15 minutes.
Blend the peppers and onion coarsely.
Heat oil in a wide pan to high heat. Sear the beef in batches until browned. Set aside.
In the same pan, fry the blended peppers for 8 minutes.
Return the beef to the pan. Toss together.
Cook for 3 more minutes on high heat until everything is glazed.
Serve immediately with rice or as a standalone dish.
